statement.p: A company set aside a certain amount of money in the year 2000. The company spent exactly {\$25{,}000} from that fund each year on perks for its employees. In 2002, there was still {\$902{,}000} left in the fund.

Let x be the number of years since 2000, and let y be the amount of money, in dollars, left in the fund that year. Use a linear equation to model the amount of money left in the fund after so many years.
A point-slope equation to model this is .
In the year 2009, there was left in the fund.
In the year , the fund will be empty.

li.p: A lines equation in point-slope form looks like y=m(x-x_0)+y_0, where m is the slope, and (x_0,y_0) is some point we know to be on the line. We first need to find the lines slope. We have been told that the account decreases by {\$25{,}000} each year, so the slope of the linear model is -25000 (dollars per year).
So now we have that y=-25000(x-x_0)+y_0. We can use (x_0,y_0)=(2,902000) to get {y = -25000\mathopen{}\left(x-2\right)+902000}.
In the year 2009, x was 9. To find the amount remaining in the fund, we substitute 9 in for x in the equation y=-25000(x-2)+902000, and we have:
So in the year 2009, the fund had {\$727{,}000} remaining in it.
The fund will be empty when it has {\$0} left in it. That is, y will equal 0. To find how many years until this happens, we substitute 0 in for y in the equation y=-25000(x-2)+902000, and we have:
Approximately 38.08 years after 2000, in the year 2038, the fund will be depleted.
